name: RNA polymerase sigma factor FliA
note: σ28 is a minor sigma factor that is responsible for initiation of transcription of a number of genes involved in motility and flagellar synthesis |CITS: [3536871][2644646][7590326][8866483]|. σ28 competes with σ70 for |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX|, binding to it more tightly than σ70 |CITS: [9209042]|. A distinguishing feature of σ28 promoters is a long -10 region (GCCGATAA); in addition, its upstream GC is highly conserved |CITS: [19400790]|. Thus, σ28 not only uses extended -10 recognition but also requires three recognition regions, -35, extended -10, and -10, for successful utilization of the core σ28 promoter |CITS: [19400790]|. Both GC and the CG motifs are functionally important in the -10 region of σ28, and this is a composite element. Based on mutational and biochemical analyses, it was shown that the upstream GC constitutes an extended -10 motif and is recognized by R91 (which recognizes both 14G and 13C), a residue in domain 3 of σ28. The downstream CG is the upstream edge of the -10 region of the promoter; two residues in region 2.4 of domain 2, D81 (which recognizes 11G) and R84 (which recognizes 12C), participate in its recognition |CITS: [19400790]|. R91, D81, and R84 are universally conserved in σ28 orthologues |CITS: [19400790]|. The dependence of σ28 on promoter sequence has been tested using the Chlamydia trachomatis hctB promoter |CITS: [16855242]|. σ28 is required for flagellin production |CITS: [6993282][6374380]|. The structural consequences of fliA mutation have been examined via electron microscopy |CITS: [7007337]|. σ28 is half as abundant as σ70 under most growth conditions, but drops in abundance following heat shock |CITS: [8808934]|. Biofilm formation depends on the level of fliA expression |CITS: [16317765][16397770]|. A global antagonistic effect on gene expression was perceived between σS, σ54, and σ28, based on transcriptome expression analysis, and this resulted in many physiological traits, including flagellum-mediated motility and utilization of nitrogen sources; as many as 60% of genes in the RpoN regulon are under reciprocal RpoS control |CITS: [21219458]|. In addition, there is a complex regulatory interaction of the three σ factors: σS, σ54, and σ28 |CITS: [21219458]|. The FliA regulon was comprehensively mapped using ChIP-seq and RNA-seq |CITS: [25275371][26379184]|. Based on transcriptional fusion of intragenic binding sites of FliA (σ28) previously identified by ChIP-seq |CITS: [25275371]|, it was determined that the most intragenic FliA binding sites are active promoters that transcribe highly unstable RNAs, and the genetic locations of intragenic FliA promoters are generally not conserved. FliA promoters are not conserved across species nor across Escherichia coli strains |CITS: [29476659]|. FliA appears not be able to induce pauses during transcription elongation in vitro |CITS: [31228934]|. Deletion of the σ finger of FliA, formed by region 3.2, decreased the overall RNAP holoenzyme activity in vitro, increasing the stability of the promoter complex |CITS: [33573428]|. However, this deletion makes the promoter complex less sensitive to the stringent response factor DksA in presence of ppGpp |CITS: [33573428]|. It was reported that RNAP holoenzyme containing FliA σ factor can incorporate NADH during transcription initiation |CITS: [33573428]|. The fliA gene expression was upregulated in a mutant deficient for the |FRAME:EG11495-MONOMER| HdeD |CITS:[34694904]|. Reviews: |CITS: [19400790][28904250]|

name: RNA polymerase sigma factor FecI
note: FecI (σ19) belongs to the subfamily of "iron starvation sigmas" within the Extracytoplasmic Function (ECF) family of sigma factors |CITS: [8596456][12207687]|. The only known target for transcription initiation by |FRAME: CPLX0-221| is the fecABCDE operon promoter |CITS: [10648550]|. This limited role is typical for the minor sigma factors. FecI was first described as a protein that regulates the expression of genes involved in the transport of ferric citrate |CITS: [2836368]| and was later shown to be the sigma factor that enables transcription from the fec transport operon promoter upstream of fecA |CITS: [2254251][7752886][7557462][8596456]|. In vitro, transcription by |FRAME: CPLX0-221| is highest at 25°C and is stimulated by the presence of potassium glutamate |CITS: [10648550]|. Three proteins, |FRAME: EG10286-MONOMER FecA| in the outer membrane, |FRAME: EG10292-MONOMER FecR| in the cytoplasmic membrane, and FecI in the cytoplasm, form a signaling pathway that responds to the presence of iron(III) dicitrate and induces expression of ferric citrate transport genes (most recently reviewed in |CITS: [31187912][17673165]|). The periplasmic N terminus of FecA interacts with the C terminus of FecR, and the cytoplasmic N terminus of FecR binds FecI |CITS: [10633096]|. Mutations in FecR can be compensated by missense mutations in the putative RNA polymerase-binding region 2.1 of FecI; these mutations don't restore the FecR-FecI interaction |CITS: [11114913]|. The FecI-FecR interaction site maps to region 4 of FecI |CITS: [12057967]|. The cytoplasmic domain of FecR stimulates the interaction of FecI with |FRAME: RPOC-MONOMER| |CITS: [12618442]|. Transcription of fecI is not autoregulated |CITS: [8602163]|, but may be upregulated by RpoS during exponential growth |CITS: [18158608]|. Under iron-replete conditions, expression of fecIR and fecABCDE is repressed by Fur-Fe2+ |CITS: [9575233][17921503]|. FecI levels appear to decrease in stationary phase cells |CITS: [10648550]|. FecI belongs to the polyamine modulon; its expression is enhanced by polyamines at the level of translation |CITS: [15326188][17220219]|. fecI mutants are unable to grow with iron(III) dicitrate as the sole source of iron |CITS: [2254251]| and do not express the fecABCDE ferric citrate transport genes |CITS: [8602163]|. Reviews: |CITS: [31187912][17673165][17370038][16718597][16428314][16333751][16102597][12732308][12207687][9148773]|

name: RNA polymerase sigma factor RpoD
note: Sigma 70 is the primary sigma factor during exponential growth, targeting |FRAME: RNAP70-CPLX| to a wide range of promoters that are essential for normal growth. Eσ70 transcribes, in the absence of any additional transcription factor, with higher efficiency genes encoding ribosomal proteins and other protein synthesis-related genes, such as rRNA- and tRNA-encoding genes and prfB, which encodes release factor 2 |CITS:[21398637]|. Sigma 70 accounts for 60-95% of the total pool of cellular sigma factors during normal exponential growth |CITS: [8808934]|. Changes away from typical growth conditions, such as heat shock or growth into stationary phase, lead to the replacement of sigma 70 with other sigma factors in the RNA polymerase holoenzyme complex |CITS: [7588636][1745227][15596728]|. When this occurs, the holoenzyme no longer recognizes the same transcription targets, leading to a decline in many RNA and protein levels, including components of the protein synthesis machinery |CITS: [7035833][3543015][12421813]|. Alternatively, under osmotic stress sigma 70 seems to be responsible for RNAP redistribution to transcribe stress-responsive genes |CITS: [30843055]|. Sigma 70 has the strongest interaction with the RNA polymerase core complex of all the known sigma factors |CITS: [10982868]|. This interaction may in fact be required for sigma 70 to bind DNA, as its amino-terminal portion normally blocks such binding in the absence of core complex |CITS: [1643661]|. When it does bind promoter DNA, sigma 70 contacts both the -10 and -35 regions simultaneously, with a binding affinity depending on the spacing between these points |CITS: [8799117]|. In promoters with a -35 site, sigma 70 is more effective when there is also a proximal half-site or a complete UP element, whereas the stationary phase sigma, |FRAME: RPOS-MONOMER|, has opposite selectivity |CITS: [15612932]|. Both sigma 70 and |FRAME: RPOS-MONOMER| compete for available core complex during stationary phase growth |CITS: [9767572]|. Sigma 70 also competes with NusA for binding to |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX|, though sigma 70's affinity for the core drops off dramatically during the change from transcriptional initiation to elongation |CITS: [1856862][10625443]|. Sigma 70 even competes with the nascent RNA strand for binding to |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX| |CITS: [10411885]|. The majority of early elongation complexes do appear to retain sigma 70 for a while, with this retention being promoted by promoter-like sequences in the initial transcribed region |CITS: [16285917]|. These sequences, bearing similarity to -10 promoters, can induce sigma 70-mediated pausing of |FRAME: RNAP70-CPLX| |CITS: [14630944][15122345]|, misincorporation-induced pausing |CITS:[28442761]|. Notably, promoters recognized by sigma 70 occur in regions rife with such promoter-like sequences |CITS: [14529615]|. Genes with promoters recognized by σ70 and not by other σ factors are weakly downregulated in stationary phase compared to exponential-phase cells growing in LB medium |CITS:[35338024]|. In vivo during stationary phase, the activity of RNA polymerase:σ70 is inhibited when it forms a complex with 6S RNA (6S RNA:RNAP:σ70), and it is released when levels of nutrients are high by accumulation of a complex of 6S RNA with a short product of RNA (6S RNA:pRNA) synthesized from 6S RNA:RNAP:σ70 |CITS: [36198425][38692564]|. Based on an XACT-seq method, cross-link-between-active-center-and-template sequencing, which directly defines the RNAP active center position relative to DNA with single-nucleotide resolution, detection and quantification of pausing in initial transcription at 411 (~4,000,000) promoter sequences in vivo showed that initial transcription pausing can occur with each nucleotide addition during initial transcription, particularly the first 4 to 5 nucleotide additions in sequences that resemble the consensus sequence element for transcription-elongaton pausing |CITS:[32750314]|. Based on nascent elongating transcript sequencing (NET-seq) combined with RNase I footprinting (RNET-seq), genome-wide identification of σ70-dependent transcription pauses in E. coli was performed |CITS: [33568644]|. In these studies, σ70 induced backtracked pauses at a 10- to 20-bp distance from many promoters |CITS: [33568644]|. Promoters with σ70-induced pauses at bp 10 to 15 have a canonical 10 element, 6- to 7-nt spacer, and YR+1Y motif, where the purine (R) corresponds to the transcription start site |CITS: [33568644]|. By contrast, promoters with σ70-induced pauses at bp 16 to 20 contain all these elements and, additionally, a 10-like sequence |CITS: [33568644]|. In vivo and in vitro analyses showed that σ70-induced backtracked pauses are relieved by Gre cleavage elongation factors |CITS: [33568644]|. The backtracking of RNAP at these pauses was determined to be of 4 to 5 bp by |CITS: [33568644]| and 2 to 4 bp by |CITS: [35653571]|. In addition to the backtracked state, the sigma-dependent pausing involves DNA scrunching with 2 to 3 bp, both at transcription initiation and transcription elongation, and the consensus sequence for sigma-dependent pausing involving DNA scrunching was determined as TP1NPYAGA+1 expressed in terms of RNAP active-center A-site and P-site positions (where N is any nucleotide and Y is a pyrimidine) |CITS: [35653571]|. Sigma 70 function is blocked by the anti-sigma factor, |FRAME: EG11738-MONOMER|. Physiologically relevant σ70 concentrations are required for reinitiation of transcription when the RNA polymerase remains bound to DNA after a round of transcription |CITS: [37459525]|. Sigma 70 has undergone significant structural analysis. A crystal structure of sigma 70 has been determined to 2.6 Å resolution, revealing that residues that bind core polymerase are on one face, with DNA-binding residues on the other |CITS: [8858155]|. Most of sigma 70 must be present to allow promoter melting |CITS: [14988563]|. Within sigma 70, there are conserved motifs responsible for binding to the -10 and -35 and other promoter regions |CITS: [2661828][2661827][14621989][8345519][12782316]|. Region 1.2 of sigma 70 is responsible for recognition of the -10-like pause regions described above, as well as recognition of the fork junction and actual promoter-proximal pausing |CITS: [17268549]|. Conserved region 3.2 of the primary σ factor (σ finger) directly contacts the template DNA strand in the open promoter complex and facilitates initiation of NTP binding in the active center of RNAP |CITS:[30321408]|. Based on a molecular beacon assay, it was determined that the σ region 3.2 decreased the rate of promoter escape and led to accumulation of inactive complexes during transcription initiation |CITS:[29101286]|. A region of sigma 70 that partially blocks the RNA exit channel in the holoenzyme is involved in binding the 3' initiating nucleotide in the active site and aids in promoter clearance |CITS: [16690607]|. A crystal structure of |FRAME: RNAP70-CPLX| has been determined to 9.5 Å resolution |CITS: [11118218]|. |FRAME: EG10893-MONOMER| interacts with sigma 70 at a subset of UP and activator-dependent promoters |CITS: [12820974]|. Interaction between |FRAME: RPOC-MONOMER| and sigma 70 promotes sigma 70 interaction with the nontemplate strand in the -10 region, while interaction between |FRAME: RPOB-MONOMER| and sigma 70 acts to block extension of nascent RNA, and thus must be disrupted to allow elongation to occur |CITS: [11439189][15761057]|. The interaction of sigma 70 with activator proteins has also been addressed in detail |CITS: [8289270]|. Sigma 70 undergoes a conformational change when it binds to the core complex, revealing the sigma factor's DNA-binding surfaces, then undergoes another change on promoter melting |CITS:[9830052][10493811][11511357]|. When σ70 is not bound to RNA polymerase (RNAP), it adopts a compact conformation due to the proximity between its R2 and R4 regions, in contrast to the extended conformation it assumes when bound to RNAP |CITS:[38957792]|. This compact conformation is predominantly observed during the stationary phase, during which alternative sigma factors are recruited for transcription |CITS:[38957792]|. This process is considered a mechanism of self-inhibition of DNA binding |CITS:[38957792]|. In in vivo and in vitro transcription analyses, E. coli RNA polymerase holoenzyme containing σ70 was intrinsically promiscuous, driving spurious transcription that was suppressed by H-NS. The promiscuity was dictated by the σ factor |CITS: [35241653]|. The residues R156 and R486 from σ70 influence the patterns of DNA opening and contribute to the transcription promiscuity |CITS: [35241653]|. A point mutation, I48S, in the poorly conserved region 1.1 of σ70 causes higher activity in protein synthesis during growth and produces a decrease of dormant cells and therefore a major cellular susceptibility to antibiotics |CITS:[32997144]|. This mutation alters the expression of at least 886 genes during exponential and stationary phases, including genes like sigma factors and their sigmulons and other transcription factors, the repression of 6S RNA (ssrS gene), which is a negative regulator of σ70, and increased rRNAs. The mutant has poor sensitivity to DksA protein |CITS:[32997144]|. A σ-competition model for RpoD based on comparative kinetic and thermodynamic properties was developed by Ganguly and Chatterji in 2012 |CITS:[22995505]|. The rpoD promoter's sensitivity to indirect regulation by σ factor competition is determined by the sequence-dependent kinetics of the rate-limiting steps of transcription initiation |CITS:[27452766] |. Under ampicillin treatment, the activity of σ70 appears to increase |CITS: [33731833]|.

name: RNA polymerase sigma factor RpoE
note: Sigma E is a minor sigma factor, specializing in responses to the effects of heat shock and other stresses on membrane and periplasmic proteins. It is regulated by a multistep protease system that sense disruptions in those proteins. Its transcriptional initiation targets include another minor sigma factor, Sigma 32. Sigma E drives transcription of a number of genes whose functions revolve around heat shock and misfolded proteins. Notably, Sigma E is induced by heat shock and is required for heat-induced transcription of rpoH, which codes for the heat-shock factor Sigma 32 |CITS: [12670975][3315851][2546916]|. Sigma E is also required for transcription of degP, which codes for a protease that degrades abnormal proteins in the periplasm |CITS: [3057437][2691330][7751307]|. Both rpoH and degP are induced at high temperature |CITS: [2691330]|. Sigma E also initiates transcription of the peptidyl-prolyl cis-trans isomerase FkpA, which also acts as a chaperone in the periplasm |CITS: [9159399]|. RpoE is a positive regulator of the prophage DLP12 lysis genes (essDp) |CITS: [25998262]|. In addition to these specific genes, a number of additional targets for Sigma E have been identified |CITS: [11274153][12900013][16336047]|. A number of cellular stresses other than heat shock also induce σE. σE is induced in response to increased expression of as well as misfolding of outer membrane proteins (OMPs) |CITS: [8276244]|. Other inducers include hyperosmotic shock, metal ion exposure, and changes in the outer membrane lipopolysaccharide (LPS) structure |CITS: [10594827][15774872][15720549]|. σE regulates extracytoplasmic functions including LPS translocation and some of its nonstoichiometric modifications |CITS:[27629414]|. σE is also induced by the starvation signal ppGpp on entry into stationary phase |CITS: [16788171]|. rpoE is a positive regulator of polyP synthesis upon nutrient limitation |CITS: [32341074][35482529]|. A novel pathway in cell lysis has been identified on the σE regulon; this pathway proceeds in a cascade that entails σE --> expression of micA and rybB small RNAs --> reduction in Omp proteins --> disintegration of the outer membrane |CITS: [22251246]|. Under ampicillin treatment, the activity of σE appears to increase |CITS: [33731833]|. rpoE expression is regulated by a multistep system that allows increased transcription in response to unassembled or misfolded proteins, in addition to initiating its own transcription at at least one of its promoters |CITS: [7889934][7889935]|. Binding of unassembled OMPs to the protease DegS stimulate its cleavage of the regulatory protein RseA |CITS: [12679035][11442831]|. The destabilized RseA is then degraded by YaeL |CITS: [12183368]|. As RseA normally tethers Sigma E to the membrane with the aid of RseB, its degradation releases Sigma E to allow it to interact with |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX| and initiate transcription |CITS: [12016219][12670975][11777003][9159522][9159523][11442831]|. Under stress, Sigma E induces the mutagenic repair of DNA breaks through a network genes that function in the activation of Sigma E. The genes that appear to be involved in the activation of Sigma E are arcA , lrp, trxB, fre, pstB,ubiX, znuC, ubiD, phoU, lipB, sdhD, fruR, pstC, cyoD, cyoA, apaH, sdhB, mdh, yigP, ygfA, nuoK, hemL, nuoG, lon, rppH, nuoH, ubiH, nuoL, nuoC, nuoJ, ubiE, and ubiA |CITS: [23224554]|. A structure of Sigme E complexed with RseA to 2 Å resolution shows that RseA sterically blocks surfaces required for binding to |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX| |CITS: [12718891]|. Sigma E is required for resistance to zinc, cadmium and copper, as well as for growth at high temperature |CITS: [15774872][7889934][7751307]|. It is also required for growth at normal temperature, but an unlinked suppressor mutation almost always occurs to allow such growth when rpoE function is lost |CITS: [9352942]|. Overexpression of Sigma E appears to prompt lysis of dead cells during stationary phase |CITS: [10960110]|. Sigma E-dependent gene expression during stationary phase has been studied |CITS: [16079349]|. Based on 60 known σE core promoters, a modular position weight matrix (PWM) model was developed to predict promoter strength and distinguish between active and inactive σE, and this model is applicable to both in vivo and in vitro measures of promoter strength. The combined -35/-10 score is the most important predictor of activity. Additionally, a conserved "AAC" motif in the -35 region is likely to be a general predictor of function for promoters recognizedby group 4 σs |CITS:[20133665]|. The structure of the complex formed by σE and its cognate -10 promoter has been solved by NMR spectroscopy and X-ray crystallography |CITS:[24531660]|. σE recognizes the 5-G-T-C-N-N-N7-3 motif (where C is -10, numbered with respect to the transcription start site). Sequence-specific recognition of C at -10 is exclusively mediated by a dynamic and variable protein loop that triggers specific recognition of the inverted nucleotide among group IV σ factors |CITS: [24531660]|. Deletion of the σ finger of σE, formed by the linker between domains 2 and 4, decreases the RNAP holoenzyme activity in vitro, increasing the stability of the promoter complex |CITS: [33573428]|. However, this deletion makes the promoter complex less sensitive to the stringent response factor DksA in presence of ppGpp |CITS: [33573428]|. It was reported that RNAP holoenzyme containing RpoE σ factor can incorporate NADH during transcription initiation |CITS: [33573428]|. SigmaE is able to induce pauses during transcription elongation in vitro when it binds to the -10-like element |CITS: [31228934]|. The first sequence-based model of a full-length bacterial promoter was proposed by Rhodius et al. in 2010 |CITS: [20133665]|. They built the model based on 60 promoters that are σE dependent and which include UP elements and core promoter sequences. The model identifies the element determinants of the promoter sequence that correlate with promoter strength and uses these features to establish a predictive model for promoter strength. The role of the UP elements in promoter strength depends both on RNAP levels and properties of the core promoter |CITS: [22156164]|. The model can also distinguish active from weak-inactive promoters |CITS: [22156164]|. Several growth conditions affect the transcription of the rpoE promoter, such as defects in LPS, the addition of ammonium metavanadate, entry into the stationary phase, shifts to high osmolarity, growth phase-dependent challenge with polymyxin B, a shift to nitrogen-limiting growth conditions, overexpression of certain genes encoding lipoproteins, and repression in glucose-supplemented medium (catabolite repression) |CITS:[27629414]|, confirming what has been reported from other studies, where rpoE activity was induced by stresses such as exposure to high osmolarity, cold shock conditions, and entry into the stationary phase |CITS:[11274153][10594827]|. Multiple transcription factors control the expression of the rpoE gene, in addition to the RpoS, RpoD, and RpoN sigma factors, which specifically respond to a variety of stimuli and cell envelope defects, including LPS alterations, and allow linking of different stress-responsive pathways |CITS:[27629414]|. Based on a biophysics in vitro model of transcription, it was shown that, like RpoD (σ70), RpoE (σE, σ24) exhibits a kinetic mix-and-match mechanism where the promoter elements complement each others binding strengths in achieving sufficient transcription activity |CITS:[28251873]|. More than 300 proteins modulate production of curli, which is involved in fundamental cellular processes such as regulation, environmental sensing, respiration, metabolism, cell envelope biogenesis, transport, and protein turnover, whereas the sigma factors, σS and σE, have opposing roles in curli production and the related processes |CITS:[29088115]|. Mutations that induce the σE or Cpx stress response system reduce curli production; on the other hand, mutant strains with increased σS levels show increased curli production. Curli fiber formation is tightly linked to key metabolic pathways, including nucleotide synthesis, cell envelope maintenance, and the citric acid cycle |CITS:[29088115]|. Reviews: |CITS: [23479440][27789741][35658528][37010795][36972330]|.

name: RNA polymerase sigma factor RpoH
note:
rpoH encodes σ32, the primary sigma factor controlling the heat shock response during log-phase growth. It is subject to tight control via a multivalent regulatory system that reponds to temperature and the abundance of misfolded proteins within the cell. σ32 levels rise, plateau and then drop following a heat shock, causing a similar induction in expression in heat shock genes |CITS: [15757896][2900239][3306410][6092838][6380765][6387714][7038687]|. At elevated temperatures, formation of Eσ32 is favoured over that of Eσ70; σ32 has greater affinity for |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX| than σ70 at high temperatures |CITS: [7588636]|. Large-scale identification of Eσ32 transcriptional targets has been carried out |CITS: [15757896][16818608]|. The consensus promoter for Eσ32 initiation is TNtCNCcCTTGAA at -35 and CCCCATtTa at -10, where lowercase letters indicate a tendency toward, but not an absolute requirement for, a certain nucleotide at that location |CITS: [3887408][19400791]|. Notably, recent work indicates that Eσ32 may only be involved in the heat shock response that occurs during log-phase, aerobic growth, with entirely different systems accounting for heat shock responses during stationary phase and anaerobic growth |CITS: [16775749]|. In addition to the interaction of σ32 to the -10 and -35 boxes of the target promoters, it interacts weakly with the spacer between them via the T128 and K130 residues |CITS: [37238608]|. It also interacts with the RNA polymerase via its σ4 and σ2 domains |CITS: [37238608]|. A histidine in σ32, instead of a tryptophan, as with σ70, works as a wedge to start the opening of DNA at the -11 and -12 positions with respect to the transcriptional start site |CITS: [37238608]|. σ32 is also responsible for genetic responses to other environmental insults. Ethanol, alkaline pH changes and hyperosmotic shock all induce the σ32-dependent heat shock response |CITS: [3539918][3542975][10594827]|. In addition, σ32 is induced by carbon starvation |CITS: [2002001]|. The regulon of σ32 also appears to be induced in the absence of the tmRNA SsrA |CITS: [36471624]|. σ32 regulation is a multivalent process consisting of transcriptional, translational and postranslational controls. rpoH is transcribed from two constitutive σ70-driven promoters, as well as at least one heat-shock responsive promoter and one σ24-driven promoter |CITS: [3315851][3323832][2546916][2691330]|. Three of the rpoH promoters are suppressed by DnaA binding |CITS: [2540187]|. rpoH transcription does not depend on σ32 |CITS: [2430947]|. σ32 abundance is regulated at the translational level via mRNA secondary structure. Two sequences in the rpoH mRNA, one near the translation start site and one internal, form a secondary structure element that is disrupted by higher temperatures, allowing translation |CITS: [2050641][1961716][7505426][9882652][10090722]|. RNAse III together with the PNPase helps to stabilize the rpoH mRNA under heat shock|CITS: [35456749]|. The transcription of rpoH also seems to be induced indirectly by RNAse III in a CytR- and CRP-independent way |CITS: [35456749]|. σ32 is regulated post-translationally via degradation and by direct inhibition of its activity |CITS: [3315848]|. Importantly, σ32 must be associated with the inner membrane to be properly regulated. Membrane localization of σ32 is facilitated by the |FRAME: SRP-CPLX "signal recognition particle (SRP) translocation system"| although σ32 does not appear to contain a signal sequence nor any transmembrane regions. Fractionation experiments suggest that approximately 50% of σ32 is membrane-associated |CITS: [24358019]|. The protein level of the heat shock sigma factor RpoH is increased due mistranslation of the general stress response sigma factor RpoS, which protects cells against heat stress. This suggests a novel regulatory role of RpoS in the heat shock response |CITS: [31419308]|. σ32 is a very unstable protein which is transiently stabilised upon heat shock |CITS: [3315848][3306410][2646289][10801971]|. σ32 is stabilized in the absence of the chaperone protein GroES and by the presence of abnormal proteins |CITS: [7912695][7916010]|. Degradation of σ32 depends on the ATP dependent protease |FRAME: CPLX0-2982 FtsH| and |FRAME: CPLX0-1163| |CITS: [7724592][9393683][9822823]|. σ32 activity is directly controlled by the |FRAME: EG10241-MONOMER "DnaK"|-DnaJ-GrpE chaperone system. When this chaperone system is occupied by substrates such as misfolded proteins, it is not available to bind σ32 and limit its activity |CITS: [8599944][9822822][9822823][1534276]|. In this way, σ32 activity is tied directly to the level of misfolded proteins in the cell. |FRAME: EG12193-MONOMER CbpA| and the |FRAME: CPLX0-3934| can also both limit σ32 activity in the same manner |CITS: [9822823][15545634]|. σ32 binds DnaK in vitro |CITS: [1565647]|; σ32 contains multiple DnaK binding sites |CITS: [24532774]|. DnaK, σ32 and DnaJ form a stable complex in vitro; efficient formation of the complex depends on the presence of ATP. DnaK and DnaJ compete with RNA polymerase core for stable binding to σ32 |CITS: [8248205]|. The chaperones DnaK, DnaJ and GrpE interact with σ32 in vivo |CITS: [1534276]|. FtsH degrades σ32 present in a DnaK-σ32-DnaJ complex in vitro but is unable to degrade σ32 present in the Eσ32 complex |CITS: [9987118]|. A σ32 I54N mutant is defective for chaperone (DnaK/J, GroEL/S) mediated inactivation in vivo, but shows no altered interaction with chaperones or RNA polymerase in vitro |CITS: [17968012]|. A σ32 I54N mutant is defective in membrane association; artificially tethering this mutant to the membrane restores homeostatic control mediated by chaperones and FtsH |CITS: [24358019]|. The hyperactive phenotype of this σ32 I54Nmutant is suppressed by Tn5 insertion into mcrC and ydbA genes, an effect that is not observed with mcrC or ydbA2 knockouts |CITS: [30531155]|. σ32 activity can also be attenuated via phosphorylation |CITS: [12657060]| and is increased under ampicillin treatment |CITS: [33731833]|. Conserved region 2 of σ32 is heavily involved in its activity and regulation. Region 2.2 is involved in interaction with the |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX|, along with other portions of the protein |CITS: [9144163][9495746]|. Region 2 is important for FtsH-mediated degradation |CITS: [11277997]|. σ32 becomes more flexible as temperature rises, and in particular region 2 appears to undergo a reversible unfolding |CITS: [14504287]|. Region 2.1 is involved in σ32 stability and its transcriptional efficacy |CITS: [15516558][15901705]|. Region C (from L118 to R140) has been implicated in binding of DnaK |CITS: [8601834]|, in high affinity binding to core RNA polymerase |CITS: [10348869]| and in FtsH mediated proteolysis |CITS: [19025566]|. Deletion of the σ finger of RpoH formed by region 3.2 decreases the overall RNAP holoenzyme activity in vitro, increasing the stability of the promoter complex |CITS: [33573428]|. However, this deletion makes the promoter complex less sensitive to the stringent response factor DksA in presence of ppGpp |CITS: [33573428]|. It was reported that RNAP holoenzyme containing RpoH σ factor can incorporate NADH during transcription initiation |CITS: [33573428]|. rpoH mutants suffer a number of ills in addition to failure of the heat shock response. They are deficient in cell division at higher temperatures and sensitive to oxidative stress at any temperature |CITS: [3529094][1729253]|. Both F and mini-F plasmid replication depend on σ32 and fail in its absence |CITS: [3526090][2447584]|. In rpoH mutants, newly synthesized protein aggregates readily even under normal growth conditions |CITS: [1936268]|. A single nucleotide substitution in rpoH (T->C) increased the abundance of this protein without changes at the transcriptional level. This mutation occurred in a successful thermal adaptation |CITS: [26373241]|. σ32 has been evaluated via linker insertion mutational analysis |CITS: [12700252]|. Whereas Eσ70-directed transcription is rifampicin sensitive, Eσ32-directed transcription is partially rifampicin resistant |CITS: [9862449]|. After cellular exposure to the aminoglycoside antibiotic kanamycin, σ32 accumulates to high levels and its degradation is apparently blocked; despite this accumulation, the response to heat shock normally induced by this σ factor does not seem to be induced under this condition |CITS: [35486612]|. On the other hand, σ32 is not detected after exposure to the antibiotics tetracycline and chloramphenicol |CITS: [35486612]|. It has been suggested that the active RpoH pathway could modulate antibiotic sensitivity |CITS: [38391529]|. A σ-competition model based on comparative kinetic and thermodynamic properties was developed |CITS: [22995505]|. σ32 appears not be able to induce pauses during transcription elongation in vitro |CITS: [31228934]|. The expression of rpoH is increased in the presence of styrene |CITS: [33640981]|. Various target genes of RpoH were found upregulated after 10 min of exposure to 2.5 mM H2O2, despite the rpoH transcription level not showing H2O2-dependent regulation |CITS:[35453340]|. Reviews: |CITS: [10791710][28904250][31281142][37010795]|
Comments: |CITS: [24358020][19400800]|.

name: RNA polymerase sigma factor RpoN
note: Sigma 54 controls expression of nitrogen-related genes, directing |FRAME: RNAP54-CPLX| to the consensus nitrogen promoter. Sigma 54 is the sigma factor controlling nitrogen-regulated and other nitrogen-related promoters |CITS: [2867543][2887548]|. It copurifies with the RNA polymerase core enzyme and binds to the consensus nitrogen promoter |CITS: [2999766][2867543]|. It is also involved in the nitric oxide stress response |CITS: [12529359]|. Mutants lacking rpoN had a significant defect in polyP synthesis |CITS: [32341074][35482529]|. During typical exponential and stationary phase growth, the amount of sigma 54 present in the cell is one tenth that of the amount of sigma 70 |CITS: [8808934]|. The metabolic context of genes dependent on sigma54 for transcription is reviewed in |CITS: [11528004]|. Sigma 54 activity is activator dependent, requiring |FRAME: PROTEIN-NRIP| or other NtrC-family transcription factors to initiate transcription. In some sigma 54-dependent promoters, DNA bending occurs, allowing |FRAME: PROTEIN-NRIP| to reach the transcription start site |CITS: [8780778]|. It has been suggested, however, that DNA bending related to sigma 54 is not as extreme as originally reported |CITS: [9790843]|. Sigma 54 has also been alternately reported to remain bound to the promoter after RNA polymerase departs during transcript elongation, or to separate from the promoter between each round of transcription |CITS: [7557383][11809874]|. The binding affinity of |FRAME: RNAP54-CPLX| for several target promoters has been evaluated |CITS: [12235394]|. Sigma 54 has a number of structural features that affect its activity. It has a pair of leucine zipper motifs that are involved in promoter melting, as well as an acidic region that has a role in the same process and a carboxy-terminal helix-turn-helix motif that is required for promoter recognition |CITS: [2203540][1447214][8107127]|. A strongly acidic hydrophobic repeat region is required for binding to RNA core polymerase |CITS: [8134358][8196043]|. Four leucines in the amino-terminal region are required for |FRAME: PROTEIN-NRIP| regulation of sigma 54 activity |CITS: [7481805][9076735]|. Sigma 54 binds to the core polymerase via a region that is very similar to sigma70 |CITS: [7592329][9427396]|. The structure of sigma 54 has been evaluated via scanning force microscopy |CITS: [9236116]|. Additional examination via x-ray scattering microscopy shows that sigma 54 and sigma 70 share broad structural similarity |CITS: [10660585]|. A 24 Å cryo-electron structure of sigma 54 holoenzyme reveals major conformational changes during DNA binding |CITS: [16246367]|. A global antagonistic effect on gene expression was perceived between σS, σ54, and σ28, based on transcriptome expression analysis, and this resulted in many physiological traits, including flagellum-mediated motility and utilization of nitrogen sources; as many as 60% of genes in the RpoN regulon are under reciprocal RpoS control |CITS: [21219458]|. In addition, there is a complex regulatory interaction of the three σ factors: σS, σ54, and σ28 |CITS: [21219458]|. The σ54 binding sites within the E. coli chromosome were comprehensively mapped using ChIP-seq |CITS: [26425847]|. A transposon insertion mutation in the rpoN gene generates cellular resistance to the antibiotic trimethoprim |CITS: [34874820]|. Reviews: |CITS: [27789741][26010401][18331472]|

name: RNA polymerase sigma factor RpoS
note: rpoS encodes the alternative sigma factor σS, a subunit of RNA polymerase that acts as the master regulator of the general stress response in E. coli. Genome-wide analyses of RpoS-dependent gene expression showed that up to 10% of the genes in E. coli are under direct or indirect control of σS |CITS: [15558318][15716429]|. RNA polymerase bound to σS efficiently transcribes genes involved in stress responses and secondary metabolism, as well as RNAs from intergenic regions with yet-unknown function(s). The effects of σS on E. coli metabolism have been investigated |CITS: [16511888]|. A lack of rpoS increases the transcription of genes belonging to high-affinity uptake systems and central metabolism, under both glucose excess and glucose-limited conditions |CITS: [26204448]|. In addition, RpoS regulates transcription of a significant fraction of genes related to sugar metabolism and polyamine metabolism in response to cellular stresses, and it also has important roles in nucleic acid synthesis, modification, and turnover |CITS: [21398637]|. RpoS is also involved in the transcription of genes related to plasmid transformation, membrane and periplasmic proteins |CITS: [22438941]|, cellular resistance to antibiotics |CITS: [34874820][38617560]|, control of hydrostatic pressure |CITS:[21705547][28819154][36737893]| and inhibition of mazEF-dependent cellular death during stationary phase |CITS: [19251848]|. Additionally, RpoS seems to be involved in phage T7 resistance |CITS: [36978471]|. A rpoS knockout mutant exhibited a fourfold increase in aerobic biofilm formation, whereas no significant effect was observed under microaerobic conditions |CITS: [36894813]|. In E. coli strains lacking the phosphoenolpyruvate:carbohydrate phosphotransferase system, RpoS acts as a controller of almost all the glycolytic genes. Additionally, genes whose products participate in ppGpp metabolism also are controlled by RpoS, although relA is not |CITS: [17938565]|. Curli fiber synthesis, which is tightly linked to key metabolic pathways, is regulated by RpoS under oxidative stress conditions |CITS: [24212724][29088115]|. The sigma factors σS and σE have opposing roles in curli production and the related processes |CITS: [29088115]|. Mutations that induce the σE or Cpx stress response system reduce curli production; on the other hand, mutant strains with increased σS levels show increased curli production. Crl, a thermosensor identified in the E. coli RpoS regulon, interacts directly with σS, promoting its binding to the csgB promoter |CITS: [14978043]|. RpoS plays a central role in survival from dehydration, protecting cells from damage during the drying phase and/or from stresses associated with the early stages of rehydration before protein expression has restarted |CITS: [29457688]|. By using ChIP-seq analysis, 63 promoter regions were found to be bound by σS in response to the transition into stationary phase, although there were not good correlations between σS binding and σS-dependent transcription. Only 38 of the 63 promoter regions (60%) recognized by the sigma factor appear to be controlled by σS |CITS: [26020590]|. Another ChIP-seq data analysis revealed that RpoS-dependent genes encode small proteins involved in resistance to oxidative stress and to osmotic stress |CITS: [26020590]|. Various target genes of RpoS were found upregulated after 10 min of exposure to 2.5 mM H2O2, despite the rpoS transcription level not showing H2O2-dependent regulation |CITS: [35453340]|. Most genes belonging to the σS regulon are also subject to additional forms of gene expression regulation |CITS: [21398637]|. Several rpoS-dependent genes are maximally expressed at the transition between the exponential and stationary phases, or even in the mid-exponential phase |CITS: [21398637]|. The activity of the RpoS iModulon decreases in cells evolved on glycerol and lactate as carbon sources |CITS:[38636729]|. The protein level of the heat shock sigma factor RpoH is increased due to protein mistranslation, and such an increase is dependent on RpoS. This suggests a novel regulatory role of RpoS in the heat shock response |CITS: [31419308]|. This sigma factor is a negative regulator in the formation of viable but nonculturable (VBNC) cells |CITS:[14510852][ 29458560]|. Polymorphisms in rpoS that affect fitness across different environments are commonly found in E. coli |CITS: [11790751][15207864][16489226]|. Promoter sequences responding to σS: σS is homologous to the housekeeping sigma factor σ70 and recognizes similar consensus promoter sequences |CITS: [11737638][14704342]|. Discrimination between σS and σ70 promoters may be achieved through their tolerance of different levels of deviation from the consensus sequence |CITS: [11737638]| and nonoptimal spacing between the -35 and -10 elements |CITS: [16420370]|. The presence and location of UP elements |CITS: [15612932]| and -35 region elements |CITS: [16420371]| may also influence usage of a subset of promoters. Based on promoter sequence comparisons, it was found that selective promoter recognition by either form of RNA polymerase can be affected by the A/T content in the -10/+1 region. Indeed, site-directed mutagenesis experiments confirmed that an A/T bias in the -10/+1 region could improve promoter recognition by σS |CITS: [21398637]|. Based on various studies, it is known that there are a set of genes with promoters that are recognized by both σ70 and σ38 (another name for σS), and these genes are referred to as σ70+38 genes. Sequence conservation was found in promoters of σ70+38 genes. It was proposed that the transcription response strength of σ70+38 genes to changes in σ38 levels can be quantitatively related to the promoter sequence |CITS:[35338024]|. RNA-seq analysis indicated that σ70+38 genes are upregulated to a similar extent as those with promoters recognized only by σ38 when in stationary phase, compared to exponential phase, whereas those genes responding only to σ70 are weakly downregulated |CITS:[35338024]|. Gene ontology analysis showed that σ70+38 genes are involved mainly in respiration, while genes with promoters recognized only by σ38 are related to metabolic processes |CITS:[35338024]|. A global antagonistic effect on gene expression was perceived between σS, σ54, and σ28, based on transcriptome expression analysis, and this effect results in many physiological traits, including flagellum-mediated motility and utilization of nitrogen sources; as many as 60% of genes in the RpoN regulon are under reciprocal RpoS control |CITS: [21219458]|. In addition, there is a complex regulatory interaction of the three σ factors σS, σ54, and σ28 |CITS: [21219458]|. A σ factor competition model based on comparative kinetic and thermodynamic properties was developed by Ganguly and Chatterji in 2012 |CITS: [22995505]|. Regulation: σS is itself subject to complex regulation at all levels (transcription, translation, activity, and protein degradation), thereby allowing integration of multiple stress signals in the cell. The regulatory mechanisms and signals are summarized below. Regulation of transcription: Transcription of rpoS is repressed by the phosphorylated response regulator ArcA |CITS: [16291649]| and during the early log phase, by the regulator CRP |CITS: [7525405]|, although a positive effect by CRP in the mid-log, late-log, and stationary phases has been reported. These findings suggest that cAMP-CRP plays a biphasic role in rpoS expression |CITS: [26260461]|. While previous reports have described a contradictory effect of CRP on rpoS at two CRP-binding sites, Ruiz Amores et al. determined, based on glucose system and gene expression analyses, that both sites have a repressive effect |CITS: [29196655]|. The BarA regulator is required for exponential-phase induction of rpoS transcription |CITS: [10931332]|. The presence of mitomycin C (MMC) induces RpoS synthesis, and the presence of BarA is necessary for the full induction of it. The principal factor for this activation is the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells exposed to MMC |CITS:[ 28468910]|. The rpoS gene is upregulated in response to ampicillin treatment |CITS:[35196813]|. rpoS transcription is directly activated by TorR_phosporylated under extreme acid conditions (pH 2.0) |CITS: [35181503]|. RpoS is induced via multiple mechanisms when under prolonged exposure to alkaline stress under semiaerobic conditions |CITS:[29802191]|. Regulatory effects of ppGpp and DksA on RpoS have been described at the levels of transcription initiation, transcription |CITS: [8253685][12193624]|, and elongation |CITS: [7642494]|, or indirectly by directly regulating the iraP and dsrA promoters, which in turn results in an increase in RpoS synthesis or stability|CITS:[29061665]|. During entry into stationary phase in complex medium, transcription of rpoS increases 5- to 10-fold |CITS: [2254248][7525405][8208244]|, but this effect is limited in defined medium |CITS: [15629914]|. On the other hand, it has been observed that polyamines increase the level of RpoS to induce glutamate-dependent acid resistance (GDAR) via gadE |CITS:[26025365]|. Based on RT-qPCR, it was shown that the gene rpoS is upregulated in the viable but nonculturable (VBNC) state that can be induced in pathogenic Escherichia coli strains by low-level chlorination exposure |CITS:[28662489]|. The expression of rpoS is also induced when the cell is exposed to the carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon nanotubes |CITS: [37317195]|. On the other hand, it has been found that rpoS expression is strongly induced in multicellular chains just before cellular adhesion to surfaces |CITS:[38814871]|. Moreover, the formation of circular multicellular communities, called rosettes, also induces the expression of this gene |CITS:[38814871]|. Regulation of mRNA stability and translation: The rpoS mRNA contains a long untranslated 5' region which plays an important role in the regulation of its stability and translation |CITS: [9721296]|. Many factors have been shown to influence formation of secondary structures, translation, and degradation of rpoS mRNA: H-NS binds to rpoS mRNA and enhances its cleavage |CITS: [15147838]|, while the DsrA small RNA stabilizes it |CITS: [10954740]|. rpoS mRNA stability during glucose starvation is diminished in the absence of |FRAME: CPLX0-3281| function |CITS: [16309817]|. Hfq is required for translation of the rpoS mRNA by promoting annealing of the DsrA small RNA to rpoS mRNA |CITS: [8654929][34571778][37116495]|. The Hfq-DsrA complex is stable until DsrA is bound to the target site in rpoS mRNA, which appears to happen immediately after the target site is transcribed |CITS: [37116495]|. On the other hand, the OxyS small RNA represses translation of rpoS, possibly by altering Hfq activity |CITS: [9774349]|. HU |CITS: [11251825]| and the RprA small RNA |CITS: [12410838]| each stimulate translation of rpoS. A review by Majdalani et al. (|CITS: [15814430]|) summarized the knowledge regarding small RNA regulators of rpoS mRNA. Starvation for phosphorus leads to accumulation of σS due to increased translation of rpoS mRNA. The upregulation is dependent on Hfq and may therefore involve an as-yet-unidentified small RNA |CITS: [14526009]|. LrhA represses translation of rpoS in an Hfq-dependent manner, which may also involve an unidentified small RNA |CITS: [16621809]|. Twenty-four nucleotides of the ribosome-binding site were shown to be necessary and sufficient for a 10-fold increase in rpoS translation |CITS: [16237004]|. A miaA insertion mutant showed decreased levels of RpoS, consistent with an MiaA requirement for efficient RpoS translation |CITS: [24296670]|. RpoS levels during growth in LB medium have been closely monitored |CITS:[35338024]|. During exponential phase, low RpoS levels are detected, and these levels increase during stationary phase |CITS:[35338024]|. Regulation of protein activity: 6S RNA activates transcription from several σS-dependent promoters while not affecting the levels of rpoS transcription or σS protein |CITS: [15262935]|. The alarmone ppGpp enhances the ability of σS to compete with σ70 for binding to core RNA polymerase |CITS: [12023304]|; the Rsd protein appears to serve a similar function |CITS: [10368152]|. Nitrogen starvation induces σS-dependent gene expression |CITS: [15716358]|, although levels of σS are only increased approximately 2-fold |CITS: [15629914]|, suggesting that nitrogen starvation acts at the level of σS activity |CITS: [16267278]|. The |FRAME: EG11092-MONOMER| enhances σS activity by increasing its ability to compete for the |FRAME: APORNAP-CPLX| |CITS: [17332743]|. Crl is a cognate enhancer of RpoS activity under different growth conditions |CITS:[23867204]|. β-Lactam antibiotics induce the RpoS regulon and increase activity |CITS: [23511474][33731833]|. Deletion of the σ finger of RpoS formed by region 3.2 decreases the overall RNAP holoenzyme activity in vitro |CITS: [33573428]|. The protein IraD appears to stabilize RpoS |CITS: [35445706]|. Under stress conditions, σS induces the mutagenic repair of DNA breaks through a network of genes that function in the activation of σS. The genes that appear to be involved in the activation of σS are cyoD, mdh, nuoC, sdhD, ubiD, apaH, cspC, cyoA, fre, glnK, hdfR, hemL, hfq, hscB, metJ, nuoG, nuoH, nuoJ, nuoK, nuoL, pgi, rbsK, sdhB, speD, speE, ubiA, ubiE, ubiH, ubiX, and yifE |CITS: [23224554]|. Protein degradation: In exponentially growing cells, the energy-dependent ClpXP protease degrades σS rapidly |CITS: [8550468]|. The RssB protein is required for rapid proteolysis of σS; it acts as a direct recognition factor, facilitating degradation of σS by the ClpXP protease |CITS: [10339606]|. |FRAME: EG10690-MONOMER| |CITS: [16556229]| as well as IraP |CITS: [16600914]|, |FRAME: EG11092-MONOMER| |CITS: [17332743]|, and H-NS |CITS: [16980505]| modulate the effect of RssB. rssB transcription is itself controlled by σS |CITS: [11566997]|. Acetyl phosphate is able to transfer a phosphate group to the Asp-58 residue of RssB and activate it |CITS: [9515704]|, although the phosphorylation level of RssB does not appear to play a role in signaling carbon starvation |CITS: [15489452]|. Carbon starvation leads to accumulation of σS due to stabilization of the protein |CITS: [9179856][15629914]|; the molecular signal has not yet been determined |CITS: [16267278]|. DnaK positively regulates the amount of RpoS through its chaperone function |CITS:[ 30271935]|, and DksA may directly or indirectly protect RpoS from degradation by ClpXP |CITS:[ 9473038]|. Selected reviews: |CITS: [12208995][12753181][12823806][15814430][15866041][15948944][16267278][17725229][19765651][21639793][31197038]| Review about the contribution of rpoS to biofilm formation |CITS: [20480211]|. Additional Reviews: |CITS: [9830102][11018136][24596257][28904250][35658528][37010795][38505546]|
